trans-unit.source: Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan
trans-unit.target: Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan

trans-unit.source: In 2023, the Sudan Red Crescent (SRCS), German Red Cross (GRC), the Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Center (RCRCCC) and the Heidelberg Institute for Geoinformation Technology (HeiGIT) worked on an Early Action Protocol (EAP) for riverine floods along the River Nile in Sudan. One of the fundamental tasks while working on an EAP is to conduct a Historical Impact Assessment (HIA), for the particular hazard. The team of HeiGIT was responsible for this particular task and this article will tell the story of how they tackled the task. Due to the outbreak of hostilities in Sudan in 2023, the team could only rely on public data from the internet and some reports and data provided by SRCS. By the end of 2023, the team had collected, 3.204 rows of data from 60 sources, covering the timeframe from 2012 to 2023.
trans-unit.target: In 2023, the Sudan Red Crescent (SRCS), German Red Cross (GRC), the Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Center (RCRCCC) and the Heidelberg Institute for Geoinformation Technology (HeiGIT) worked on an Early Action Protocol (EAP) for riverine floods along the River Nile in Sudan. One of the fundamental tasks while working on an EAP is to conduct a Historical Impact Assessment (HIA), for the particular hazard. The team of HeiGIT was responsible for this particular task and this article will tell the story of how they tackled the task. Due to the outbreak of hostilities in Sudan in 2023, the team could only rely on public data from the internet and some reports and data provided by SRCS. By the end of 2023, the team had collected, 3.204 rows of data from 60 sources, covering the timeframe from 2012 to 2023.

trans-unit.source: Why is a Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) Important?
trans-unit.target: Why is a Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) Important?

trans-unit.source: A HIA has two purposes. First, understanding in detail what kind of problems are caused by a particular hazard, allows people to make informed decisions on the selection of early actions to counter those problems. Secondly, without a good understanding of which magnitude of flood causes significant humanitarian impact, one can not adjust trigger levels accordingly to tackle those significant events.
trans-unit.target: A HIA has two purposes. First, understanding in detail what kind of problems are caused by a particular hazard, allows people to make informed decisions on the selection of early actions to counter those problems. Secondly, without a good understanding of which magnitude of flood causes significant humanitarian impact, one can not adjust trigger levels accordingly to tackle those significant events.

trans-unit.source: Conduct an HIA to gain a detailed understanding of the impact of past flood events on the highest spatial and temporal resolution possible.
trans-unit.target: Conduct an HIA to gain a detailed understanding of the impact of past flood events on the highest spatial and temporal resolution possible.

trans-unit.source: What were the main problems and challenges we faced while conducting the HIA?
trans-unit.target: What were the main problems and challenges we faced while conducting the HIA?

trans-unit.source: The one BIG challenge in this case was that there are no good datasets on flood impacts in Sudan. Practically, all information is scattered across a huge number of reports, maps, tables, dashboards and newspaper articles. Such documents can be found for example on ReliefWeb.
trans-unit.target: The one BIG challenge in this case was that there are no good datasets on flood impacts in Sudan. Practically, all information is scattered across a huge number of reports, maps, tables, dashboards and newspaper articles. Such documents can be found for example on ReliefWeb.

trans-unit.source: __Challenge 2: How to get diverse data in one easy-to-use data format__
trans-unit.target: __Challenge 2: How to get diverse data in one easy-to-use data format__

trans-unit.source: Since all information about flood impacts is contained in such a diverse range of documents and formats, it is difficult to bring the information together in one dataset.
trans-unit.target: Since all information about flood impacts is contained in such a diverse range of documents and formats, it is difficult to bring the information together in one dataset.

trans-unit.source: __Challenge 3: Differentiating between riverine flood impacts and flash flood impacts__
trans-unit.target: __Challenge 3: Differentiating between riverine flood impacts and flash flood impacts__

trans-unit.source: Riverine and flash floods often occur at the same time in the same place, so it is almost impossible to state what was the exact cause of the impact. And even when there was a single flash flood event, in reports it is often referred to as a flood. Hence, there is the real risk that impacts caused by flash floods are listed as river flood impacts.
trans-unit.target: Riverine and flash floods often occur at the same time in the same place, so it is almost impossible to state what was the exact cause of the impact. And even when there was a single flash flood event, in reports it is often referred to as a flood. Hence, there is the real risk that impacts caused by flash floods are listed as river flood impacts.

trans-unit.source: Key concept of our methodology
trans-unit.target: Key concept of our methodology

trans-unit.source: Our goal was to store all available impact information in one table or dataset. If we would use a classic weight table we would end up with a super clumsy table since there are so many different types of impacts a flood can cause. To avoid that we decided to use a long table. The advantages of a long table over a classic wider table in this context are:
trans-unit.target: Our goal was to store all available impact information in one table or dataset. If we would use a classic weight table we would end up with a super clumsy table since there are so many different types of impacts a flood can cause. To avoid that we decided to use a long table. The advantages of a long table over a classic wider table in this context are:

trans-unit.source: Long tables can accommodate a variable number of attributes, making them suitable for diverse datasets.
trans-unit.target: Long tables can accommodate a variable number of attributes, making them suitable for diverse datasets.

trans-unit.source: They can handle large datasets efficiently, making them suitable for compiling extensive information.
trans-unit.target: They can handle large datasets efficiently, making them suitable for compiling extensive information.

trans-unit.source: Simplifies the integration of data from various sources, providing a consistent structure.
trans-unit.target: Simplifies the integration of data from various sources, providing a consistent structure.

trans-unit.source: Many analysis tools work well with long-format data, making it easier to analyze and derive insights.
trans-unit.target: Many analysis tools work well with long-format data, making it easier to analyze and derive insights.

trans-unit.source: Step by Step HIA Sudan
trans-unit.target: Step by Step HIA Sudan

trans-unit.source: The Area of Interest (AOI) for the HeiGIT team was formally limited to Khartoum, Northern, White Nile and Sennar. However, the analysis and data collection were conducted for all states of Sudan.
trans-unit.target: The Area of Interest (AOI) for the HeiGIT team was formally limited to Khartoum, Northern, White Nile and Sennar. However, the analysis and data collection were conducted for all states of Sudan.

trans-unit.source: We want to tie single pieces of information like impacts, to known flood events in Sudan. Thus we first need a comprehensive list of such events. In the case of Sudan, there are two sources, EM-DAT and ReliefWeb. EM-DAT is a disaster database that lists events above a certain severity. ReliefWeb is actually an information platform for humanitarian response. But it has a list of active and past disasters as well. Both databases list the same events for the most part. By comparing the two datasets and only selecting unique events, we receive a list of all significant flood events in Sudan and the timeframes of all events.
trans-unit.target: We want to tie single pieces of information like impacts, to known flood events in Sudan. Thus we first need a comprehensive list of such events. In the case of Sudan, there are two sources, EM-DAT and ReliefWeb. EM-DAT is a disaster database that lists events above a certain severity. ReliefWeb is actually an information platform for humanitarian response. But it has a list of active and past disasters as well. Both databases list the same events for the most part. By comparing the two datasets and only selecting unique events, we receive a list of all significant flood events in Sudan and the timeframes of all events.

trans-unit.source: EM-Dat lists 21 flood events in Sudan from 2000 to 2024
trans-unit.target: EM-Dat lists 21 flood events in Sudan from 2000 to 2024

trans-unit.source: Relief Web lists 29 events between 1988 and 2024
trans-unit.target: Relief Web lists 29 events between 1988 and 2024

trans-unit.source: In total, there were 35 flood events reported between 1988 and 2021 In some cases, the start and end dates cannot precisely be identified. This is not a problem, we only want to have a rough overview about when there have been floods.
trans-unit.target: In total, there were 35 flood events reported between 1988 and 2021 In some cases, the start and end dates cannot precisely be identified. This is not a problem, we only want to have a rough overview about when there have been floods.

trans-unit.source: Now we search for datasets and other information for each of the flood events identified in step 2. Naturally, it will be much easier to find information on more recent events. This process can take a lot of time and sometimes needs multiple iterations.
trans-unit.target: Now we search for datasets and other information for each of the flood events identified in step 2. Naturally, it will be much easier to find information on more recent events. This process can take a lot of time and sometimes needs multiple iterations.

trans-unit.source: There are three principal sources of datasets. The Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS), ReliefWeb and Floodlist.
trans-unit.target: There are three principal sources of datasets. The Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS), ReliefWeb and Floodlist.

trans-unit.source:
Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS)
SRCS has documentation about flood impacts and past operations. Furthermore, SRCS was able to source some datasets from the Sudan National Council for Civil Defense (NCCD) and the Sudan Humanitarian Aid Commission (HAC).
trans-unit.target:
Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS)
SRCS has documentation about flood impacts and past operations. Furthermore, SRCS was able to source some datasets from the Sudan National Council for Civil Defense (NCCD) and the Sudan Humanitarian Aid Commission (HAC).

trans-unit.source:
ReliefWeb
ReliefWeb is the largest database for humanitarian reports and bulletins. The datasets are very conveniently organized based on specific flood events. This makes it easy to match information with an event.
trans-unit.target:
ReliefWeb
ReliefWeb is the largest database for humanitarian reports and bulletins. The datasets are very conveniently organized based on specific flood events. This makes it easy to match information with an event.

trans-unit.source:
Flood List
Floodlis is a news portal all about floods. The articles are ordered chronologically.
trans-unit.target:
Flood List
Floodlis is a news portal all about floods. The articles are ordered chronologically.

trans-unit.source: Of course, much information from the different sources is redundant. A significant part of the work is to sift through the datasets and identify unique information.
trans-unit.target: Of course, much information from the different sources is redundant. A significant part of the work is to sift through the datasets and identify unique information.

trans-unit.source: We use the term data set to refer to all types of publications from which we can obtain data. In the table below we list typical datasets used in the Sudan HIA. Remember, we can pull all kinds of information out of a dataset. It does not have to be quantitative or in table format or anything like that. It can also be info directly from texts or graphics.
trans-unit.target: We use the term data set to refer to all types of publications from which we can obtain data. In the table below we list typical datasets used in the Sudan HIA. Remember, we can pull all kinds of information out of a dataset. It does not have to be quantitative or in table format or anything like that. It can also be info directly from texts or graphics.

trans-unit.source: [Sudan Rapid Post Disaster Needs and Recovery Assessment (Rapid PDNRA)](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-rapid-post-disaster-needs-and-recovery-assessment-rapid-pdnra)
trans-unit.target: [Sudan Rapid Post Disaster Needs and Recovery Assessment (Rapid PDNRA)](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-rapid-post-disaster-needs-and-recovery-assessment-rapid-pdnra)

trans-unit.source: Tables; Graphics; Maps; Info directly from texts
trans-unit.target: Tables; Graphics; Maps; Info directly from texts

trans-unit.source: [Sudan – North Darfur Town Devastated by Rains, Flash Floods](https://floodlist.com/africa/sudan-north-darfur-floods-october-2019)
trans-unit.target: [Sudan – North Darfur Town Devastated by Rains, Flash Floods](https://floodlist.com/africa/sudan-north-darfur-floods-october-2019)

trans-unit.source: In general, we can use any kind of dataset e.g. reports, maps, bulletins, tables…. However, we do not need to check all datasets available for one event. The selection of datasets is based on two principal parameters.
trans-unit.target: In general, we can use any kind of dataset e.g. reports, maps, bulletins, tables…. However, we do not need to check all datasets available for one event. The selection of datasets is based on two principal parameters.

trans-unit.source: The assumption is that later after the event, the picture of what happens becomes clearer, thus the data report is more reliable. This means., if you have multiple similar datasets containing similar data, chose the newer one.
trans-unit.target: The assumption is that later after the event, the picture of what happens becomes clearer, thus the data report is more reliable. This means., if you have multiple similar datasets containing similar data, chose the newer one.

trans-unit.source: In this example, the later map from the [13th of November](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-situation-report-13-nov-2020-enar) shows more affected people for instance in Central Darfur the the map published on the [9th of November](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-situation-report-9-nov-2020-enar).
trans-unit.target: In this example, the later map from the [13th of November](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-situation-report-13-nov-2020-enar) shows more affected people for instance in Central Darfur the the map published on the [9th of November](https://reliefweb.int/report/sudan/sudan-situation-report-9-nov-2020-enar).

trans-unit.source: It is important to not only use data from one source because of confidence. <!--what confidence--> The assumption is that different organisations have different capacities and work in different areas, thus they may have better information in some locations of sectors than other organisations.
trans-unit.target: It is important to not only use data from one source because of confidence. <!--what confidence--> The assumption is that different organisations have different capacities and work in different areas, thus they may have better information in some locations of sectors than other organisations.

trans-unit.source: When selecting datasets it is important to save the datasets on your computer. To be able to later retrace which piece of information was taken from which datasets, we have to give the datasets unique identification codes. In the Sudan project, we used the simple structure year of the flood the document is referring to (for example 2019), the publishing organisation (for example IFRC) and the publishing date (for example `20191003`).
trans-unit.target: When selecting datasets it is important to save the datasets on your computer. To be able to later retrace which piece of information was taken from which datasets, we have to give the datasets unique identification codes. In the Sudan project, we used the simple structure year of the flood the document is referring to (for example 2019), the publishing organisation (for example IFRC) and the publishing date (for example `20191003`).

trans-unit.source: At the end of the selection process, you should have multiple relevant datasets with IDs for every event, covering all relevant areas and sectors.
trans-unit.target: At the end of the selection process, you should have multiple relevant datasets with IDs for every event, covering all relevant areas and sectors.

trans-unit.source: Before we can start compiling data, we need to prepare the table structure we will use. The table needs to accommodate four components. __Date__, __data source__, __location__,and __impact information__.
trans-unit.target: Before we can start compiling data, we need to prepare the table structure we will use. The table needs to accommodate four components. __Date__, __data source__, __location__,and __impact information__.

trans-unit.source: The date information must accommodate the flood event information from the list of flood events prepared in Step 2. And potential dates of specific impact information. The date portion of the whole Excel table would look like this:
trans-unit.target: The date information must accommodate the flood event information from the list of flood events prepared in Step 2. And potential dates of specific impact information. The date portion of the whole Excel table would look like this:

trans-unit.source: This section is simply one column with the ID of the dataset from which the particular information was taken.
trans-unit.target: This section is simply one column with the ID of the dataset from which the particular information was taken.

trans-unit.source: Practical all impact information refers to state, locality, town or refugee/IDP camp level. This means we need a column for each of these levels and one column to indicate the level the information is referring to. In this way, we can later filter for all information on for example locality level.
trans-unit.target: Practical all impact information refers to state, locality, town or refugee/IDP camp level. This means we need a column for each of these levels and one column to indicate the level the information is referring to. In this way, we can later filter for all information on for example locality level.

trans-unit.source: In the Sudan HIA, most of the information has been on the state level, whereas the team found very little information on the camp level.
trans-unit.target: In the Sudan HIA, most of the information has been on the state level, whereas the team found very little information on the camp level.

trans-unit.source: The actual impact information consists of two parts. One part is always the impact type. This explain what happened. For example, people were affected by the flood, the cholera broke out or schools got damaged.
trans-unit.target: The actual impact information consists of two parts. One part is always the impact type. This explain what happened. For example, people were affected by the flood, the cholera broke out or schools got damaged.

trans-unit.source: The other part is either the impact quantity or the impact quality. It can not be both! The impact quantity describes simply how many of something. How many people have been affected? How many schools got damaged?
trans-unit.target: The other part is either the impact quantity or the impact quality. It can not be both! The impact quantity describes simply how many of something. How many people have been affected? How many schools got damaged?

trans-unit.source: The impact quality is used if something cannot be described with numbers but with "Yes" or "No". For example, there was a disease outbreak of cholear. Cholera is not a number. Yes there was cholera outbreak. Or a locality was affected -> Yes the locality was affected.
trans-unit.target: The impact quality is used if something cannot be described with numbers but with "Yes" or "No". For example, there was a disease outbreak of cholear. Cholera is not a number. Yes there was cholera outbreak. Or a locality was affected -> Yes the locality was affected.

trans-unit.source: Hence we need three columns to describe impacts: impact_typ, impact_quality and impact_quantity.
trans-unit.target: Hence we need three columns to describe impacts: impact_typ, impact_quality and impact_quantity.

trans-unit.source: It makes sense to list some of the basic impact types we are interested in or which are very commonly reported. Such as affected people or deaths. The list of impact types can be extended on the fly. It is however important to stay consistent. The HeiGIT team used 75 different impact types. You can find the whole list below.
trans-unit.target: It makes sense to list some of the basic impact types we are interested in or which are very commonly reported. Such as affected people or deaths. The list of impact types can be extended on the fly. It is however important to stay consistent. The HeiGIT team used 75 different impact types. You can find the whole list below.

trans-unit.source: Now we have your final table structure. We can put ALL information from the selected datasets we deem relevant into this table structure thus creating a consistent historical impact dataset.
trans-unit.target: Now we have your final table structure. We can put ALL information from the selected datasets we deem relevant into this table structure thus creating a consistent historical impact dataset.

trans-unit.source: During the data compilation, we simply identify the relevant information in the dataset and transfer it into the table. Below you can find some examples of the process.
trans-unit.target: During the data compilation, we simply identify the relevant information in the dataset and transfer it into the table. Below you can find some examples of the process.

trans-unit.source: This is a small extract from Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018 page 4.
trans-unit.target: This is a small extract from Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018 page 4.

trans-unit.source: __SUDAN FLOOD RESPONSE HUMANITARIAN PARTNERS UPDATE BY STATE #3 (as of 19 Oct, 2020)__
trans-unit.target: __SUDAN FLOOD RESPONSE HUMANITARIAN PARTNERS UPDATE BY STATE #3 (as of 19 Oct, 2020)__

trans-unit.source: The map on the first page shows affecte population per state. This information can be extracted.
trans-unit.target: The map on the first page shows affecte population per state. This information can be extracted.

trans-unit.source: The quote below is just one bit of relevant information from the flood list article.
trans-unit.target: The quote below is just one bit of relevant information from the flood list article.

trans-unit.source: Compiling the data in EXCEL is a time-intensive and repeatable task. Here are some tips to speed up the process:
trans-unit.target: Compiling the data in EXCEL is a time-intensive and repeatable task. Here are some tips to speed up the process:

trans-unit.source: Try to use the copy function of Excel as much as possible. When taking info from a map, the information in the columns, year, start_date, end_date, date, Source_ID, and admin_level usually stay the same. So you can just copy and paste this information. In most cases, you should only write the name of location, Impact_type, Impact_quality and Impact_quantity.
trans-unit.target: Try to use the copy function of Excel as much as possible. When taking info from a map, the information in the columns, year, start_date, end_date, date, Source_ID, and admin_level usually stay the same. So you can just copy and paste this information. In most cases, you should only write the name of location, Impact_type, Impact_quality and Impact_quantity.

trans-unit.source: If you encounter tables that do not fit into your table structure use ChatGPT to turn them into a long table format. For example, look at the image in the table below.
trans-unit.target: If you encounter tables that do not fit into your table structure use ChatGPT to turn them into a long table format. For example, look at the image in the table below.

trans-unit.source: You can copy the content which produces this text:
trans-unit.target: You can copy the content which produces this text:

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} DREF Final Report Sudan: Floods 31 May 2013
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} DREF Final Report Sudan: Floods 31 May 2013

trans-unit.source: Go to ChatGPT and use a prompt like „Can you turn this information taken from a Table into a long table format?“ + the table as text
trans-unit.target: Go to ChatGPT and use a prompt like „Can you turn this information taken from a Table into a long table format?“ + the table as text

trans-unit.source: Probably you will have to ask ChatGPT to give you the whole table. Something like: "Super good! Can you give me a table on the other states as well? "
trans-unit.target: Probably you will have to ask ChatGPT to give you the whole table. Something like: "Super good! Can you give me a table on the other states as well? "

trans-unit.source: Google Gemini Can also handle images. For example, it can turn this graph into a table which is much easier to handle than copying everything by hand.
trans-unit.target: Google Gemini Can also handle images. For example, it can turn this graph into a table which is much easier to handle than copying everything by hand.

trans-unit.source: The resulting table can easily be adjusted in Excel.
trans-unit.target: The resulting table can easily be adjusted in Excel.

trans-unit.source: When creating such a dataset there will be errors like misspellings of names or wrong numbers. To address that you will need to clean the dataset before actually using it. To clean the dataset you can use Excel or specialized tools like [OpenRefine](https://openrefine.org/).
trans-unit.target: When creating such a dataset there will be errors like misspellings of names or wrong numbers. To address that you will need to clean the dataset before actually using it. To clean the dataset you can use Excel or specialized tools like [OpenRefine](https://openrefine.org/).

trans-unit.source: Independent of which tool you will use, here are some important points you have to check when cleaning the data:
trans-unit.target: Independent of which tool you will use, here are some important points you have to check when cleaning the data:

trans-unit.source: Check if all values are within the expected range of years.
trans-unit.target: Check if all values are within the expected range of years.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid year values becaus we need a year information for all data
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid year values becaus we need a year information for all data

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" or "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in the Year column. Use the "Text filter" or "Numeric filter" to filter out any rows with invalid or missing year values.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" or "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in the Year column. Use the "Text filter" or "Numeric filter" to filter out any rows with invalid or missing year values.

trans-unit.source: Check if all dates are in the correct date format.
trans-unit.target: Check if all dates are in the correct date format.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no inconsistent date values.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no inconsistent date values.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Edit cells" > "Common transforms" > "To date" option to convert date columns to a standard date format. Use the "Text facet" to identify and correct any inconsistencies in formatting or missing dates.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Edit cells" > "Common transforms" > "To date" option to convert date columns to a standard date format. Use the "Text facet" to identify and correct any inconsistencies in formatting or missing dates.

trans-unit.source: Check that there is only one way of spelling for every individual source_ID!
trans-unit.target: Check that there is only one way of spelling for every individual source_ID!

trans-unit.source: Check if all source IDs follow a consistent naming convention.
trans-unit.target: Check if all source IDs follow a consistent naming convention.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing source IDs.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing source IDs.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on `Cluster` and set `Method` to `Key collision`or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on `Cluster` and set `Method` to `Key collision`or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: Check if all administrative units are correctly categorized.
trans-unit.target: Check if all administrative units are correctly categorized.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no misspelled or inconsistent administrative unit names.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no misspelled or inconsistent administrative unit names.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Consolidate the of states and loclities that they are consisten with the list in the [location chapter](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_historical_impact_assessment_sudan.html#location).Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Consolidate the of states and loclities that they are consisten with the list in the [location chapter](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_historical_impact_assessment_sudan.html#location).Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: Check if all impact types are correctly categorized and named.
trans-unit.target: Check if all impact types are correctly categorized and named.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no duplicate or missing impact types.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no duplicate or missing impact types.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: Check if all numerical values are within expected ranges.
trans-unit.target: Check if all numerical values are within expected ranges.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid numerical values.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid numerical values.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in each numerical column. Use the "Edit cells" or "Transform" option to handle missing or invalid values, and standardize formatting.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in each numerical column. Use the "Edit cells" or "Transform" option to handle missing or invalid values, and standardize formatting.

trans-unit.source: Check that the same impacts are spelt consistently. For example illness names.
trans-unit.target: Check that the same impacts are spelt consistently. For example illness names.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: It might be the case that you have for one flood event, multiple information on for example affected populations for the same location. This could later be a problem when you want to analyse the data. Probably, the reddened information is from two different sources. So you can delete the information of the older source and only keep the one from the up-to-date source as possible.
trans-unit.target: It might be the case that you have for one flood event, multiple information on for example affected populations for the same location. This could later be a problem when you want to analyse the data. Probably, the reddened information is from two different sources. So you can delete the information of the older source and only keep the one from the up-to-date source as possible.

trans-unit.source: Step 7: Adding P-Codes to the table
trans-unit.target: Step 7: Adding P-Codes to the table

trans-unit.source: Create a new empty Excel file and name it `Sudan_impact_p_code`.
trans-unit.target: Create a new empty Excel file and name it `Sudan_impact_p_code`.

trans-unit.source: Open the new Excel file and click on the Data tab. Click on `Data -> Get Data -> From File -> From Excel Workbook` and select your cleaned impact data file from the previous section `HIA Data Cleaning`. We will enrich the information of this table by adding the P-Codes.
trans-unit.target: Open the new Excel file and click on the Data tab. Click on `Data -> Get Data -> From File -> From Excel Workbook` and select your cleaned impact data file from the previous section `HIA Data Cleaning`. We will enrich the information of this table by adding the P-Codes.

trans-unit.source: The `Navigator` Window will open. Select the relevant Excel sheet. Click on the drop-down menu `Load -> Load To...`. The `Import Data` Window will open. Here select `Only Create Connection`.
trans-unit.target: The `Navigator` Window will open. Select the relevant Excel sheet. Click on the drop-down menu `Load -> Load To...`. The `Import Data` Window will open. Here select `Only Create Connection`.

trans-unit.source: Repeat the previous two steps for the file `sdn_adminboundaries_tabulardata.xlsx`. Select the sheet `ADM1`. The data can be downloaded from HDX containing the [Subnational Administrative Boundaries of Sudan](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ab-sdn?). Here we can find information about the different administrative levels of Sudan beginning with 0 (country), 1 (state), and 2 (district). Make sure to download the xlsx file.
trans-unit.target: Repeat the previous two steps for the file `sdn_adminboundaries_tabulardata.xlsx`. Select the sheet `ADM1`. The data can be downloaded from HDX containing the [Subnational Administrative Boundaries of Sudan](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ab-sdn?). Here we can find information about the different administrative levels of Sudan beginning with 0 (country), 1 (state), and 2 (district). Make sure to download the xlsx file.

trans-unit.source: Once you have loaded both files you should see the `Queries & Connections` panel on the right-hand side of your Excel. The panel should show the impact sheet and the ADM1 sheet.
trans-unit.target: Once you have loaded both files you should see the `Queries & Connections` panel on the right-hand side of your Excel. The panel should show the impact sheet and the ADM1 sheet.

trans-unit.source: Now, click on the `Data tab -> Get Data -> Combine Queries -> Merge`. The window `Merge` should open.
trans-unit.target: Now, click on the `Data tab -> Get Data -> Combine Queries -> Merge`. The window `Merge` should open.

trans-unit.source: In the `Merge` window, select the `admin_1` column for the impact dataset and the `ADM1_EN` for the ADM1 table. Click on these columns to mark them green.
trans-unit.target: In the `Merge` window, select the `admin_1` column for the impact dataset and the `ADM1_EN` for the ADM1 table. Click on these columns to mark them green.

trans-unit.source: Under `Join Kind` select `Left Outer (all from first, matching from second)`. This will take all rows from the impact dataset and the matching data from the ADM1 table. The information below should show a green check and `The selection matches [number of rows] of [number of rows] rows from the first table`. If not, some of your state names are not consistent with the ones in the ADM1 file. In this case, you would need to do more data cleaning and make sure that naming conventions are met. Click Ok.
trans-unit.target: Under `Join Kind` select `Left Outer (all from first, matching from second)`. This will take all rows from the impact dataset and the matching data from the ADM1 table. The information below should show a green check and `The selection matches [number of rows] of [number of rows] rows from the first table`. If not, some of your state names are not consistent with the ones in the ADM1 file. In this case, you would need to do more data cleaning and make sure that naming conventions are met. Click Ok.

trans-unit.source: The new window `Merge1 – Power Query Editor` will open. Click on the icon next to ADM1 (column header). Select only the columns you want to keep. We recommend to only keep `ADM1_EN` and most importantly `ADM1_PCODE`. Uncheck `Use original column names as prefix` and click OK.
trans-unit.target: The new window `Merge1 – Power Query Editor` will open. Click on the icon next to ADM1 (column header). Select only the columns you want to keep. We recommend to only keep `ADM1_EN` and most importantly `ADM1_PCODE`. Uncheck `Use original column names as prefix` and click OK.

trans-unit.source: The table preview should now show your entire impact table with the columns `ADM1_EN` and `ADM1_PCODE` on the right end of the table. Click on `Close & Load`.
trans-unit.target: The table preview should now show your entire impact table with the columns `ADM1_EN` and `ADM1_PCODE` on the right end of the table. Click on `Close & Load`.

trans-unit.source: The result should be in the Excel file `Sudan_impact_p_code`. The file should contain all the information from the original impact table and the two new columns `ADM1_EN` and `ADM1_PCODE` with this important information for later operations.
trans-unit.target: The result should be in the Excel file `Sudan_impact_p_code`. The file should contain all the information from the original impact table and the two new columns `ADM1_EN` and `ADM1_PCODE` with this important information for later operations.

trans-unit.source: Step 8: Data export from Excel to QGIS
trans-unit.target: Step 8: Data export from Excel to QGIS

trans-unit.source: Now we have our impact dataset cleaned and ready to use. Since the dataset is a long table we cannot use it as a normal table in Excel. To get the data in a format so we can use it in QGIS or create a graph out of it, we need to pivot it. Depending on your work with impact quantity or quality, there are small differences. Here is how you do it:
trans-unit.target: Now we have our impact dataset cleaned and ready to use. Since the dataset is a long table we cannot use it as a normal table in Excel. To get the data in a format so we can use it in QGIS or create a graph out of it, we need to pivot it. Depending on your work with impact quantity or quality, there are small differences. Here is how you do it:

trans-unit.source: Impact Quantity for one year on state level:
trans-unit.target: Impact Quantity for one year on state level:

trans-unit.source: Turn the data in a table by clicking on `Insert` -> `Table`-> check `My table has headers`
trans-unit.target: Turn the data in a table by clicking on `Insert` -> `Table`-> check `My table has headers`

trans-unit.source: Also under the `Insert`-Tab click on `Pivot Table`. Make sure your table range is correct. Check `New Worksheet`. Click `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Also under the `Insert`-Tab click on `Pivot Table`. Make sure your table range is correct. Check `New Worksheet`. Click `OK`.

trans-unit.source: Setup the pivot table by placing the columns as follows:
trans-unit.target: Setup the pivot table by placing the columns as follows:

trans-unit.source: Rows: admin_1 or admin_1_PCODE (If you want to use this table in QGIS, you should use admin_1_PCODE Instead of admin_1)
trans-unit.target: Rows: admin_1 or admin_1_PCODE (If you want to use this table in QGIS, you should use admin_1_PCODE Instead of admin_1)

trans-unit.source: To see the sum of the different impacts click on Impact_quantity under Values -> `Value Field Settings` -> select `Sum`.
trans-unit.target: To see the sum of the different impacts click on Impact_quantity under Values -> `Value Field Settings` -> select `Sum`.

trans-unit.source: Directly above the pivot table, you should see the option to filter by year. Select the year you are interested in. For the following example the year 2020 was used.
trans-unit.target: Directly above the pivot table, you should see the option to filter by year. Select the year you are interested in. For the following example the year 2020 was used.

trans-unit.source: Now you can just copy the whole table, and place it in a new worksheet. Make sure to only paste the values. Save this output as a CSV-file, this will make the import of the subset into QGIS easier. Now we can use this table to join it with an existing geodataset in QGIS.
trans-unit.target: Now you can just copy the whole table, and place it in a new worksheet. Make sure to only paste the values. Save this output as a CSV-file, this will make the import of the subset into QGIS easier. Now we can use this table to join it with an existing geodataset in QGIS.

trans-unit.source: Step 9: Data analysis in QGIS
trans-unit.target: Step 9: Data analysis in QGIS

trans-unit.source: Visualise impact quantity data for one year on state level in QGIS
trans-unit.target: Visualise impact quantity data for one year on state level in QGIS

trans-unit.source: Import the previously created CSV file into QGIS. Open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here you can input your CSV-file and depending on the `File Format` you need to define Costum delimiters or you can just select CSV. Always check the Sample Data output at the bottom to see if the import is working as expected. You propably will also need to check the `Record and Fields Options` and specify if your first record is a header or already data. Lastly, it is important to specify the `Geometry Definition`, were you can just select `No geometry`. An example will be shown in {numref}`HIA_csv_import`.
trans-unit.target: Import the previously created CSV file into QGIS. Open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here you can input your CSV-file and depending on the `File Format` you need to define Costum delimiters or you can just select CSV. Always check the Sample Data output at the bottom to see if the import is working as expected. You propably will also need to check the `Record and Fields Options` and specify if your first record is a header or already data. Lastly, it is important to specify the `Geometry Definition`, were you can just select `No geometry`. An example will be shown in {numref}`HIA_csv_import`.

trans-unit.source: For this example we will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan. Make sure that your geodata has the admin_1_PCODE column that will be used for joining the table data with the geodata. We will use the tool `Join attributes by field value`. And select the corresponding columns. This is shown in {numref}`HIA_join` below.
trans-unit.target: For this example we will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan. Make sure that your geodata has the admin_1_PCODE column that will be used for joining the table data with the geodata. We will use the tool `Join attributes by field value`. And select the corresponding columns. This is shown in {numref}`HIA_join` below.

trans-unit.source: The information can now be visualized on a spatial scale and maps can be created to transport important information. An example could be to visualize the total affected population for the year of 2020 on state level. But we can also visualize more specific impact types such as damaged schools or damaged sanitation. This is how such a map could look like.
trans-unit.target: The information can now be visualized on a spatial scale and maps can be created to transport important information. An example could be to visualize the total affected population for the year of 2020 on state level. But we can also visualize more specific impact types such as damaged schools or damaged sanitation. This is how such a map could look like.

trans-unit.source: Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years
trans-unit.target: Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years

trans-unit.source: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.
trans-unit.target: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.

trans-unit.source: In the first step we will create a new `pivot table` and store it in a new sheet. Here we will specify the following:
trans-unit.target: In the first step we will create a new `pivot table` and store it in a new sheet. Here we will specify the following:

trans-unit.source: Now we want to filter the columns labels to the following impact types:
trans-unit.target: Now we want to filter the columns labels to the following impact types:

trans-unit.source: We also want to include the Event and Flash-floods as these types can also be associated with flooding events as a result of heavy rainfall, seasonal rainfall, etc. We also want to make sure that we rename the column containing the sum of the flooding events to `Sum flood events`.
trans-unit.target: We also want to include the Event and Flash-floods as these types can also be associated with flooding events as a result of heavy rainfall, seasonal rainfall, etc. We also want to make sure that we rename the column containing the sum of the flooding events to `Sum flood events`.

trans-unit.source: Now we export this excel sheet as a CSV file and import it into QGIS. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).
trans-unit.target: Now we export this excel sheet as a CSV file and import it into QGIS. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).

trans-unit.source: The next step follows the same logic as step 2 in the previous example. We will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan and join them with the imported CSV data. We will use the `Join attributes by field value` tool and select the ADM1_PCODE as the table field used for the join. An example is shown in {numref}`HIA_join_floods`.
trans-unit.target: The next step follows the same logic as step 2 in the previous example. We will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan and join them with the imported CSV data. We will use the `Join attributes by field value` tool and select the ADM1_PCODE as the table field used for the join. An example is shown in {numref}`HIA_join_floods`.

trans-unit.source: Now we can visualise our results using `Graduated` and selecting the corresponding column of the attribute table `Sum flood events`. Select an appropriate color scheme and start creating your map. Your final product could look like {numref}`HIA_map_floods`.
trans-unit.target: Now we can visualise our results using `Graduated` and selecting the corresponding column of the attribute table `Sum flood events`. Select an appropriate color scheme and start creating your map. Your final product could look like {numref}`HIA_map_floods`.

trans-unit.source: We can expand this analysis by also including the affected population an calculate the average number of affected population per flood event on state level.
trans-unit.target: We can expand this analysis by also including the affected population an calculate the average number of affected population per flood event on state level.

trans-unit.source: We continue with creating a new `pivot table`. We will do the following specifications:
trans-unit.target: We continue with creating a new `pivot table`. We will do the following specifications:

trans-unit.source: Again we will apply a filter to the column labels and select the impact type `Pop_affected`. Also make sure to rename the Grand total to `Sum affected pop`. Export the subset as a CSV file.
trans-unit.target: Again we will apply a filter to the column labels and select the impact type `Pop_affected`. Also make sure to rename the Grand total to `Sum affected pop`. Export the subset as a CSV file.

trans-unit.source: The next steps in QGIS will follow the same logic as before. Import the data and join the table onto your output from the previous task. This will look the following {numref}`HIA_join_aff_pop`.
trans-unit.target: The next steps in QGIS will follow the same logic as before. Import the data and join the table onto your output from the previous task. This will look the following {numref}`HIA_join_aff_pop`.

trans-unit.source: Now we will calculate the the average number of affected population per flood event on state level. To do so, we need to activate the editing mode for our geodata clicking on this symbol ![](/fig/mActionToggleEditing.png). In the next step we will open the `Field calculator` ![](/fig/mActionCalculateField.png). Here we will calculate the sum of the affected population divided by the number of flood events for each state. Your calculation will look like {numref}`HIA_field_calculator`.
trans-unit.target: Now we will calculate the the average number of affected population per flood event on state level. To do so, we need to activate the editing mode for our geodata clicking on this symbol ![](/fig/mActionToggleEditing.png). In the next step we will open the `Field calculator` ![](/fig/mActionCalculateField.png). Here we will calculate the sum of the affected population divided by the number of flood events for each state. Your calculation will look like {numref}`HIA_field_calculator`.

trans-unit.source: Visualise the results and create a map.
trans-unit.target: Visualise the results and create a map.
